title: Drama
paragraphs: Drama is a performing art that portrays real or imagined events through the enactment of scripted dialogue before an audience. Theatre is the traditional and most prominent medium of drama, although it has expanded to include film, television, and radio. Drama conveys ideas, emotions, and human experiences through artistic performance. Classical drama is commonly classified into five principal genres: comedy, tragedy, farce, melodrama, and musical drama.
focus: Drama as a narrative form across theatre, film, and television.
reference: Balme, C.B. (2008) The Cambridge Introduction to Theatre Studies. Cambridge: Cambridge University Press.
Kennedy, D. (ed.) (2010) The Oxford Companion to Theatre and Performance. Oxford: Oxford University Press.
lens: Cultural studies, narrative theory, and media form analysis.

title: Film
paragraphs: Film (or cinema) is an audiovisual art form that uses a sequence of moving images with sound to construct narrative and create the illusion of continuous time and space. It emerged in the late nineteenth century in Europe and the United States and has developed into a global industry, with major production centers such as Hollywood and Bollywood.

While films are primarily produced for entertainment, they also carry intellectual and symbolic meanings, including political and cultural dimensions. Film reception extends beyond narrative comprehension to interpretation of underlying ideas, which is the focus of film studies in terms of form, context, and impact.
focus: Film as a visual and narrative medium.
reference: Balio, T. (2010) The Oxford History of World Cinema. Oxford: Oxford University Press.
Bordwell, D. and Thompson, K. (2016) Film Art: An Introduction. 11th edn. New York: McGraw-Hill Education.
lens: Film studies, semiotics, and cultural analysis.

title: Television
paragraphs: Television is a system for transmitting visual and audio content to screens for entertainment, information, and education. It has become a central feature of households and institutions and serves as a major medium for advertising and cultural communication.

Since the mid-twentieth century, television has experienced rapid expansion and significant social influence, becoming a shared experience across diverse audiences. It has also evolved technologically from early mechanical systems to modern electronic broadcasting, with continuing changes in viewing practices today.
focus: Television as a cultural and industrial medium shaping contemporary media consumption.
reference: Biagi, S. (2005) Media/Impact: An Introduction to Mass Media. Belmont, CA: Thomson Wadsworth.
Campbell, R., Martin, C.R. and Fabos, B. (2012) Media Essentials: A Brief Introduction. Boston: Bedford/St. Martin’s.

title: Soap Opera
paragraphs: A soap opera is a serial dramatic genre broadcast on television or radio, focusing on the lives of multiple characters, with emphasis on emotional relationships and domestic themes in a melodramatic style. It is characterized by continuous storytelling with no fixed ending, allowing it to run for many years and reach thousands of episodes.

The genre originated in the United States in the early 1930s within radio broadcasting. Early examples include Painted Dreams (1930) and Clara, Lu, 'n Em (1931). The term “soap opera” comes from soap and detergent advertising that sponsored these programs, which targeted daytime audiences, particularly housewives.
focus: Long-form serial television drama and its role in shaping emotional engagement and everyday storytelling.
reference: Cantor, M.G. and Pingree, S. (1983) The Soap Opera. Beverly Hills: Sage Publications.
Allen, R.C. (1985) Speaking of Soap Operas. Chapel Hill: University of North Carolina Press.

title: Telenovela
paragraphs: A telenovela is a Latin American melodramatic television serial and one of the most popular formats in the region, often dominating prime-time schedules and generating high advertising revenues. It has also been widely exported to international markets.
Although telenovelas share certain features with soap operas, they are considered a distinct genre with a defined narrative structure and a specific cultural identity.
focus: Latin American serial television and its global circulation as a cultural export.
reference: La Pastina, A.C. and Straubhaar, J.D. (2005) 'The Centrality of Telenovelas in Latin American Culture', Media, Culture & Society.
Allen, R.C. (1995) To Be Continued… Soap Operas Around the World. London: Routledge.

title: Dizi
paragraphs: Dizi refers to Turkish television drama series, including both comedy and drama formats. Since the early 2000s, Turkish television dramas have experienced rapid growth and have become significant cultural and economic exports.

Turkey is now one of the fastest-growing exporters of television series, with its productions widely distributed across Asia, Europe, Latin America, and North Africa, contributing to its global cultural influence.
focus: Turkish television drama as a global media export and cultural form.
reference: Özçetin, B. (2019) 'The Making of a Cultural Export: Turkish TV Dramas', Journal of Communication Inquiry.
Buccianti, A. (2010) 'Turkish Television Series and Their Global Impact', Middle East Journal of Culture and Communication.
lens: Global media studies and cultural circulation theory.

title: Podcast
paragraphs: Podcasting is an emerging digital audio medium based on on-demand audio storytelling distributed via online platforms. It enables creators to produce both serialized and standalone content that enhances audience engagement through spoken narrative and conversational formats. Podcasts are primarily accessed through platforms such as Spotify and Apple Podcasts and have become an integral component of contemporary digital media consumption, particularly within participatory and independent media production contexts.
focus: Study of podcasting as an emerging audio-visual and cultural medium.
reference: Berry, R. (2016) 'Podcasting: Considering the Evolution of the Medium', In Llinares, D., Fox, N. and Berry, R. (eds.) Podcasting: New Aural Cultures and Digital Media. Palgrave Macmillan.
Llinares, D., Fox, N. and Berry, R. (eds.) (2018) Podcasting: New Aural Cultures and Digital Media. Cham: Springer.
lens: Media studies, sound studies, and audience reception theory.

title: AI
paragraphs: Artificial intelligence (AI) in media refers to the use of computational systems and algorithms in film production, storytelling, and media analysis. It enables the creation of AI-generated content and the emergence of algorithmic forms of storytelling that reshape traditional narrative structures. AI also raises issues related to digital and intellectual authorship, as creative processes become distributed between humans and machines. In addition, it introduces ethical concerns regarding originality and creative control within contemporary media production.
focus: Exploration of artificial intelligence in film production, storytelling, and media analysis.
reference: Floridi, L. (2014) The Fourth Revolution: How the Infosphere is Reshaping Human Reality. Oxford: Oxford University Press.
Jenkins, H. (2006) Convergence Culture: Where Old and New Media Collide. New York: New York University Press.
Cave, S., Craig, C., & Dihal, K. (2019) Scary Robots: AI in Popular Culture. Oxford University Press.
lens: Media theory, digital humanities, and film studies.
